Here we derived the expression for Γ QP S by means of the real time technique without employing the ImF -method [60].
Making use of the above results, evaluating the Green functions (144) and keeping in mind the detailed balance condition
div.formula: P x,x (ω) = e ω T P x,x (-ω)(151)
div.p: we obtain
div.formula: V = Φ 0 Lvγ 2 QP S 4 ς 2 IΦ 0 2 sinh IΦ 0 2T ,(152)
div.p: where
div.formula: ς(ω) = τ λ 0 (2πT ) λ-1 Γ λ 2 -iω 2πT Γ λ 2 + iω 2πT Γ(λ) . (153
div.p: Here for the sake of simplicity we assumed that vτ 0 ∼ x 0 . It is satisfactory to observe that the result (152), (153) matches with that derived in Ref. [12] by means of the ImF -technique.
A detailed analysis of the relation between the ImF -approach and the Keldysh technique employed here can be found in Ref. [62].
Let us now turn to the voltage-voltage correlator. Our perturbative analysis allows to recover three different contributions to the noise power spectrum, i.e.
div.formula: S Ω = dte iΩt V (t)V (0) = S (0) Ω + S r Ω + S a Ω .(154)
div.p: The first of these contributions S
Ω defines equilibrium voltage noise for a transmission line and has nothing to do with QPS. This contribution reads
div.formula: S (0) Ω = iΩ 2 coth Ω 2T 16e 2 G R ϕϕ (Ω) -G R ϕϕ (-Ω) .(155)
div.p: The remaining two terms are due to QPS effects. The term S r Ω is also proportional to coth Ω 2T and contains the products of two retarded (advanced) Green functions:
div.formula: S r Ω = γ 2 QP S Ω 2 coth Ω 2T 16e 2 L 0 dx L 0 dx Re G R ϕχ (x; Ω) ×(F x,x (Ω)G R ϕχ (x ; Ω) -F x,x (0)G R ϕχ (x; Ω)) ,(156)
div.p: where
div.formula: F x,x (Ω) = -P x,x (Ω + IΦ 0 ) -P x,x (Ω -IΦ 0 ) + Px,x (-Ω + IΦ 0 ) + Px,x (-Ω -IΦ 0 ). (157
div.p: The remaining term S a Ω contains the product of one retarded and one advanced Green functions and scales with the combinations
div.formula: C ± = coth Ω±IΦ 0 2T -coth Ω 2T as S a Ω = γ 2 QP S Ω 2 32e 2 L 0 dx L 0 dx G R ϕχ (x; Ω)G R ϕχ (x ; -Ω) × ± C ± (P x,x (Ω ± IΦ 0 ) -P x,x (-Ω ∓ IΦ 0 )) . (158
div.p: Eqs. ( 154)-( 158) together with the expressions for the Green functions fully account for the voltage noise power spectrum of a superconducting nanowire in the perturbative in γ QP S regime.
In the zero bias limit I → 0 the term S a Ω vanishes, and the equilibrium noise spectrum
div.formula: S Ω = S (0) Ω +S r
div.p: Ω is determined from FDT, see also [36]. At non-zero bias values the QPS noise turns non-equilibrium. In the zero frequency limit Ω → 0 the terms S (0) Ω and S r Ω vanish, and the voltage noise S Ω→0 ≡ S 0 is determined solely by S a Ω . Then from Eq. (158) we get [59]
div.formula: S 0 = Φ 2 0 (Γ QP S (IΦ 0 ) + Γ QP S (-IΦ 0 )) = Φ 0 coth IΦ 0 2T V ,(159)
div.p: where V is specified in Eqs. ( 149), (152). Combining Eq. ( 159) with Eqs. ( 152), (153) we obtain

At higher temperatures T IΦ 0 (though still T ∆ 0 ) Eq. ( 160) just describes equilibrium voltage noise S 0 = 2T R of a linear Ohmic resistor R = V /I ∝ T 2λ-3 [12]. In the opposite low temperature limit T IΦ 0 it accounts for QPS-induced shot noise S 0 = Φ 0 V obeying Poisson statistics with an effective "charge" equal to the flux quantum Φ 0 . This result makes the physical origin of shot noise in superconducting nanowires transparent: It is produced by coherent tunneling of magnetic flux quanta Φ 0 across the wire, cf. also Fig. 7.
Another interesting limit is that of sufficiently high frequencies and/or long wires v/L Ω ∆ 0 . In this case we obtain
div.formula: S (0) Ω = λ 8πe 2 Ω coth Ω 2T (Ω/2E C ) 2 + (λ/π) 2 . (161
div.p: Note that this contribution is independent of the wire length L. At low T and Ω/λ E C = e 2 /2C we have S

Ω ∝ 1/Ω, i.e. in a certain regime the wire may generate 1/f voltage noise. Evaluating the QPS terms S r Ω and S a Ω we observe that the latter scales linearly with the wire length L whereas the former does not. Hence, the term S r Ω can be disregarded in the long wire limit. For the remaining QPS term S a Ω we get
div.formula: S a Ω = Lλ 2 vγ 2 QP S 8e 2 ς IΦ 0 2 -Ω -ς IΦ 0 2 + Ω sinh IΦ 0 2T ς IΦ 0 2 ((Ω/2E C ) 2 + (λ/π) 2 ) sinh Ω 2T .(162)
div.p: At T → 0 from Eq. ( 162) we find

This result can be interpreted as follows. At T = 0 each QPS event excites (at least) two plasmons with total energy E = IΦ 0 and zero total momentum propagating in the opposite directions along the wire. One plasmon (which energy equals to E/2) gets dissipated at the grounded end of the wire while another one (also with energy E/2) reaches its opposite end causing voltage fluctuations (emits a photon) with frequency Ω measured by a detector.
Clearly, at T = 0 this process is only possible at Ω < E/2 in the agreement with Eq. ( 163).

The result (162) is also illustrated in Fig. 11. At sufficiently small Ω (though still we keep Ω v/L) we observe a non-monotonous dependence of S Ω on temperature which serves as a clear manifestation of quantum coherent nature of QPS noise.

To conclude our analysis of QPS-induced shot noise in superconducting nanowires, we point out that the perturbative in γ QP S approach employed here is fully justified in the "superconducting" regime, i.e. for not too thin wires with λ > 2. In wires with λ < 2 (characterized by unbound QPS-anti-QPS pairs) the perturbation theory becomes obsolete at low enough energies and large distances since γ QP S gets effectively renormalized to higher values, see, e.g., BKT-like RG equations (115). Even in this case, however, our results may still remain applicable at relatively high temperature, frequency and/or current values. In the low energy limit long wires with λ < 2 show an insulating behavior, as follows from the exact solution of the corresponding sine-Gordon model [61]. This solution suggests that also voltage fluctuations become large in this limit.
Further interesting features of shot noise of the voltage in superconducting nanowires under different measuring schemes were analyzed in Refs. [43,[62][63][64].

div.head: VII. FULL COUNTING STATISTICS OF QUANTUM PHASE SLIPS
div.p: In order to fully describe voltage fluctuations in the system under consideration it is in general necessary to evaluate all cumulants of the voltage operator. Various aspects of voltage fluctuation statistics were already discussed in the case of quasi-one-dimensional wires [17] and resistively shunted Josephson junctions [65,66]. The authors [17,65,66] restricted their analysis to thermal fluctuations and, hence, their results remain applicable only at sufficiently high temperatures. Here, in contrast, we will set up a fully quantum mechanical treatment of the problem that essentially operates with interacting quantum phase slips and allows to fully describe full counting statistics of voltage fluctuations at any temperature down to T → 0. In our further analysis we will to a large extent follow Ref. [67].

As in Sec. VIA, we can again make use of the Josephson relation between the voltage and the phase variables. Then, analogously to the averages in Eqs. ( 139) and ( 143), we can express the general correlator of voltages in the form
Here we stress that Eq. ( 164) defines the symmetrized voltage correlators. E.g., for n = 2 this equation is equivalent to Eq. ( 149), whereas for n = 3 one can verify that [62,68]
where T and T are, respectively, the forward and backward time ordering operators.

div.head: A. Cumulant generating function
div.p: In order to proceed with our calculation of higher voltage correlators it will be convenient for us to define the cumulant generating function
div.formula: W[J] = ln(Z[J]) = ln e i dtJ(t)V (t) ,(166)
div.p: where

is a voltage drop across the wire and ... denotes the quantum average fulfilled with the total Hamiltonian of our system. Evaluating the N -th variational derivative of W[J] with respect to J(t) one recovers the N -th cumulant of the voltage operator.

The function Z[J] can be conveniently derived with the aid of Keldysh path integral technique already described in Sec. VI. Defining the voltage values V (167) on both forward and backward time branches of the Keldysh contour, respectively V F and V B , and introducing, as before, "classical" and "quantum"
div.formula: variables v + = (V F + V B )/2 and V -= V F -V B , one
div.p: can verify that the voltage cumulants of (164) are exactly equivalent to the cumulants of the "classical" variable v + (t) in our path integral formalism. With this in mind the function Z[J] can be expressed as
div.formula: Z[J] = e iS QP S [χ + ,χ -] e i dtJ(t)v + (t) 0 ,(168)
div.p: where S QP S is defined in Eq. ( 140). The function (168) generates voltage correlators
Let us eliminate the second exponent in Eq. ( 168) by making a linear substitution χ i = λ i + χi and imposing the condition
div.formula: χi e i dtJ(t)v + (t) 0 = 0 (170) implying that λ + (x, t) = χ 0 (x, t) -dt G K χv (x; t, t )J(t ),(171)
λ -(x, t) = -dt G A χv (x; t, t )J(t ). (172
div.p: Here we denoted χ 0 ≡ χ + 0 and introduced both Keldysh and advanced Green functions
div.formula: G K χv (x; t, t ) = -i χ + (x, t)v + (t ) 0 (173
div.p: and
The latter function coincides with the transposed version of the retarded Green function
div.formula: G R χv (x; t, t ) = -i χ + (x, t)v -(t ) 0 . (175
div.p: As a result of the above manipulations, we get
div.formula: Z[J] = e -i 2 dtdt J(t)G K vv (t,t )J(t ) e iS QP S [λ + + χ+ ,λ -+ χ-] 0 ,(176)
div.p: where Keldysh Green function G K vv (t, t ) is defined analogously to that in Eq. ( 173). The remaining average can be performed with the aid of the Wick's theorem and expressed via the two Green functions,
div.formula: G K χχ (x, x ; t, t ) = -i χ+ (x, t) χ+ (x , t ) 0 , (177
div.p: while all averages of the type χ-χ-0 vanish identically due to causality.
Let us now evaluate the cumulant generating function by expanding Z[J] up to the second order in γ QP S . We obtain
div.formula: W[J] = - i 2 dtdt J(t)G K vv (t, t )J(t ) + i S QP S [λ + + χ+ , λ -+ χ-] 0 - 1 2 S 2 QP S [λ + + χ+ , λ -+ χ-] 0 + 1 2 S QP S [λ + + χ+ , λ -+ χ-] 2 0 . (179
div.p: instructive to define the time average

where the time interval τ exceeds any relevant time scale for our problem. It is easy to demonstrate that the cumulants of v are identical to the corresponding cumulants of the voltage operator evaluated in the zero frequency limit. For instance, for the first two cumulants one readily finds
where S ω (I) is the frequency dependent voltage noise power for our wire already evaluated in Sec. VI.
In order to derive the cumulant generating function for v
div.formula: w(j) = ln e ijv(185)
div.p: it suffices to make use of Eq. ( 180) and set J(t) = j/τ for -τ /2 < t < τ /2 and J(t) = 0 otherwise. For large enough time intervals τ the combination λ + (x, t) -χ 0 (x, t) becomes practically independent of both x and t implying that λ + (x, t) -λ + (x , t ) ≈ IΦ 0 (t -t ).
Employing the equation of motion
we obtain
div.formula: lim ω→0 G A χv (x; ω) = lim ω→0 G R vχ (x; ω) = Φ 0 (187)
div.p: and, hence, we have λ -(x, t) ≈ -Φ 0 j/τ . As a result we get
div.formula: w(j) τ = - ij 2 2τ 2 G K vv (0)- γ 2 QP S 2 sin Φ 0 j τ L/2 -L/2 dxdx ∞ 0 dt P (x, x ; t)-P (x , x; -t) sin(IΦ 0 t) -γ 2 QP S sin 2 Φ 0 j 2τ L/2 -L/2 dxdx ∞ 0 dt P (x, x ; t) + P (x , x; -t) cos(IΦ 0 t). (188
div.p: Performing the Fourier transformation
div.formula: P (x, x ; ω) = ∞ 0 dte iωt P (x, x ; t)(189)
div.p: and introducing
div.formula: Γ(ω) = γ 2 QP S 4 L/2 -L/2 dxdx P (x, x ; ω) + P * (x , x; ω)(190)
div.p: we find
div.formula: w(j) τ = - ij 2 2τ 2 G K vv (0) + Γ(IΦ 0 ) e iΦ 0 j τ -1 + Γ(-IΦ 0 ) e -iΦ 0 j τ -1 . (191
div.p: This expression fully describes the statistics of QPS-induced voltage fluctuations in superconducting nanowires in the zero frequency limit.

It follows immediately from Eq. ( 191) that this statistics is Poissonian in the above limit. In particular, combining Eqs. ( 185) and ( 191) and evaluating the first and the second derivatives of w with respect to j, we again recover the first two voltage cumulants in Eqs.
Higher voltage cumulants in the zero frequency limit can be found in the same manner.
Let us define them as
div.formula: C N (I) = (-i) N τ N -1 ∂ N j w(j) j→0 . (192
div.p: After a simple algebra all zero frequency cumulants can be expressed through the currentvoltage characteristics for our system. In particular, for odd cumulants one has
whereas for even ones we obtain
div.formula: C 2N (I) = Φ 2N -1 0 V (I) coth IΦ 0 2T . (194
div.p: The above results demonstrate that in the long time limit the effect of interacting QPS reduces to that of independent sharp voltage pulses which occur with the effective rate Γ(IΦ 0 ) and are described by Poisson statistics. Note that this conclusion holds only for not too thin wires with λ > 2 described by quantum phase slips bound in close pairs.
C. Noise power in the short wire limit
At non-zero frequencies the system behavior becomes more involved and the statistics of voltage fluctuations deviates from Poissonian, as it will be demonstrated below.
The general expression for the noise power is defined as
div.formula: S ω (I) = -dte iωt δ 2 W[J] δJ(t)δJ(0) J→0 . (195
div.p: Making use of Eq. ( 180) we find
div.formula: S ω (I) = iG K vv (ω) + γ 2 QP S 2 L/2 -L/2 dxdx G K vχ (x; ω)G R vχ (x ; ω) ∞ 0 dt P (x, x ; t) -P (x , x; -t) cos(IΦ 0 t) e iωt -1 + 1 4 L/2 -L/2 dxdx G R vχ (x; ω)G R vχ (x ; -ω) ∞ -∞ dt P (x, x ; t)+P (x , x; -t) cos(IΦ 0 t)e iωt +{ω → -ω} . (196
div.p: With the aid of FDT this result can be transformed to that already derived in Sec. VI where we merely addressed the long wire limit. Here, in contrast, we will specify the expression for the noise power for shorter wires. This limit also includes the case of Josephson junctions and other types of short superconducting weak links.

Let us note that each term in the square brackets in Eq. ( 196) contains the combination of the form-factors P (x, x ; ω) describing intrinsic dynamics of a superconducting nanowire during the QPS process, as well as two Green functions of the vχ-type indicating how the detector "feels" voltage fluctuations inside the nanowire. Provided the wire is short enough one can retain only the dependence of these Green functions on frequency and ignore their spatial coordinates. Then one gets
where τ R is the effective RC-time of the system. Accordingly we obtain
div.formula: G K vχ (x, ω) ≈ -iωτ R coth(ω/(2T ))(198)
div.p: Employing these approximations, from Eq. ( 196) we obtain
div.formula: S ω (I) = iG K vv (ω) -iΦ 2 0 τ R ω coth ω 2T Γ R (ω + IΦ 0 ) + Γ R (ω -IΦ 0 ) + Γ R (-ω + IΦ 0 ) + Γ R (-ω -IΦ 0 ) -2Γ R (IΦ 0 ) -2Γ R (-IΦ 0 ) + 1 2 Φ 2 0 Γ(ω + IΦ 0 ) + Γ(ω -IΦ 0 ) + Γ(-ω + IΦ 0 ) + Γ(-ω -IΦ 0 ) , (199
div.p: where we introduced the function
div.formula: Γ R (ω) = γ 2 QP S 4 L/2 -L/2 dxdx P (x, x ; ω) -P * (x , x; -ω) (200) related to Γ(ω) (190) as Γ R (ω) = dz 2πi Γ(z) -Γ(-z) z -ω -i0 . (201
div.p: For an illustration let us consider a short superconducting nanowire embedded in a linear dissipative external circuit modeled by an Ohmic shunt resistor R S . This situation is equally relevant for resistively shunted Josephson junctions in the limit of large Josephson coupling energies E J . In this limit one has
div.formula: G R χχ (x, x ; ω) ≈ - 2πiµ ω + i0 , (202
div.p: where µ = R Q /R S is the shunt dimensionless conductance and R Q = π/(2e 2 ) is the "superconducting" resistance quantum. The QPS rate then equals to
div.formula: Γ(ω) = γ 2 QP S (2πT τ R ) 2µ e ω 2T Γ µ + iω 2πT Γ µ -iω 2πT 8πT Γ(2µ) ,(203)
div.p: where Γ(y) is the Euler gamma-function and τ -1 R plays the role of effective high-energy cutoff frequency. Evaluating the corresponding integrals in the short wire limit ω, T, IΦ 0 τ -1 R and also for 1 < µ < 3/2, we obtain
div.formula: Γ R (ω) = const -iΓ(ω)e -ω 2T sin πµ + iω 2T cos(πµ) . (204
div.p: These expressions can be simplified in certain limits. For example, by setting 0 < µ -1 1
we get
div.formula: Γ(ω) ≈ γ 2 QP S (2πT τ R ) 2µ e ω 2T -2C(µ-1) ω 2 + 4π 2 T 2 (µ -1) 2 16πT 2 Γ(2µ) sin πµ + iω 2T sin πµ + iω 2T , (205
div.p: where C is Euler-Mascheroni constant. Also the expressions for both QPS rates get significantly simplified in the limit |ω| T . One has
div.formula: Γ(ω) ≈ πγ 2 QP S θ(ω) (ωτ R ) 2µ 2ωΓ(2µ) , (206
) Γ R (ω) ≈ const + πγ 2 QP S |ωτ R | 2µ e -iπµsign(ω) 4ωΓ(2µ) cos(πµ) . (207
div.p: Accordingly, in the zero-temperature limit one finds
div.formula: C N (I) = πγ 2 QP S sign N (I) Φ N +2µ-1 0 τ 2µ R 2Γ(2µ) |I| 2µ-1 . (208
div.p: The above results are consistent with ones previously derived for ultrasmall Josephson junctions [69].

div.head: D. Higher voltage cumulants
div.p: We now turn to higher voltage cumulants at non-zero frequencies. We adopt the following definition for the N -th voltage cumulant:
In the zero frequency limit this definition yields S 00...0

Under the condition T, ω τ -1 R or, in other words, provided the detector immediately "feels" QPS-generated voltage fluctuations, one can set τ R → 0 and explicitly evaluate all voltage cumulants at non-zero frequencies. In this case the cumulant generating function takes the form
div.formula: W[J] ≈ - i 2 dtdt J(t)G K vv (t -t )J(t ) -γ 2 QP S L/2 -L/2 dxdx dt t dt P (x, x ; t -t ) -P (x , x; t -t) × sin IΦ 0 (t -t ) sin Φ 0 J(t) 2 cos Φ 0 J(t ) 2 - γ 2 QP S 2 L/2 -L/2 dxdx dt dt P (x, x ; t -t ) + P (x , x; t -t) × cos IΦ 0 (t -t ) sin Φ 0 J(t) 2 sin Φ 0 J(t ) 2 .(211)
div.p: One can observe that the second term in Eq. ( 211) can only contribute to odd cumulants, whereas the last term determines all even cumulants. After some algebra we arrive at the following expressions for both even and odd voltage cumulants, respectively
and
where the sum is taken over all permutations of frequencies.
The above results allows us to extend the relation between the voltage cumulants and the current-voltage characteristics of our device to non-zero frequencies. For the odd cumulants we obtain
while the expression for the even cumulants reads
These expressions can be evaluated numerically with the aid of Eqs. ( 203)-(207) for Γ(ω)

and Γ R (ω) derived above. The corresponding results for the third voltage cumulant as a function of two frequencies are displayed in Fig. 12 at low and high temperatures. The third voltage cumulant generally consists of real and imaginary parts
div.formula: S ω 1 ,ω 2 (I) = ReS ω 1 ,ω 2 (I) + iImS ω 1 ,ω 2 (I).(216)
div.p: As could be seen in the plots, both these functions become considerably smoother at higher T .

To conclude our analysis of voltage fluctuation statistics in superconducting nanowires we emphasize again that in the zero-frequency limit the this statistics reduces to Poissonian similarly to the situation encountered in a number of other tunneling-like problems. In this limit all (symmeterized) cumulants of the voltage operator can be expressed in a simple manner through the current-voltage characteristics of the system V (I), cf. Eqs. (193), (194).

At non-zero frequencies, quantum voltage fluctuations in superconducting nanowires are not anymore described by Poisson statistics. This is because inter-QPS interaction produced by an effective environment (due to the wire itself and/or an external dissipative circuit) starts playing a more important role at shorter time scales making the whole problem much more involved. Remarkably, also in this case it is possible to establish a relation between the voltage cumulants and the current-voltage characteristics of our device V (I), though in a

ReS ω1ω2 (I) S 00 (I) much more complicated form as compared to that in the zero frequency limit, cf. Eqs. (214), (215). The latter observation could be important for possible experimental verification of the above results.

div.head: VIII. TOPOLOGY-CONTROLLED PHASE COHERENCE IN SUPERCONDUCTING NANOWIRES
div.p: It is by now well established that at T → 0 a long superconducting nanowire suffers a quantum phase transition (QPT) controlled by the wire cross section s or, equivalently by the parameter λ ≡ g/8 which we already introduced above and which sets the magnitude of (logarithmic in space-time) interaction between different quantum phase slips [12]. For λ > 2 this interaction is strong enough and close QPS-anti-QPS pairs are formed in the wire which then demonstrates vanishing linear resistance R ∝ T 2λ-3 , cf. Eqs. ( 152), (153).

Hence, as long as λ > 2 (or, equivalently, g > 16) the ground state of the system can be considered superconducting. In contrast, for λ < 2 inter-QPS interaction is weak, quantum phase slips are unbound, and the wire acquires non-zero resistance which tends to increase with decreasing T . The latter feature allows one to call the wire behavior insulating provided λ < 2. Thus, at g = 16 and T → 0 one expects a superconductor-to-insulator quantum phase transition (SIT) to occur in the systems under consideration.

Let us emphasize that possible insulating behavior of superconducting nanowires is essentially linked to a certain type of experiment performed with such nanowires and may not always be realized. For example, an ultrathin superconducting nanowire forming a closed ring does not loose the ability to carry supercurrent even for λ < 2, as we have already demonstrated in Sec. V. In this case a characteristic length scale (118) emerges beyond which phase coherence (and, hence, supercurrent) gets exponentially suppressed by QPS. In what follows
div.formula: R c ∼ L c ∝ exp ag ξ 2 -λ(217)
div.p: emerges beyond which phase coherence (and, hence, supercurrent) gets exponentially suppressed by QPS. The correlation length (217) diverges at λ → 2, thus signaling the transition to the ordered phase λ > 2 with bound QPS-anti-QPS pairs and more robust superconductivity.

On the other hand, a closed ring geometry seriously restricts the space for phase fluctuations, thereby enhancing the tendency towards superconductivity, see, e.g., Ref. [70] for further discussion of this point. For this reason it is highly desirable to analyze ground state properties of superconducting nanowires where no fluctuation configurations are suppressed by geometry constraints and/or boundary conditions. An example of the system of that kind is depicted in Fig. 13. A long superconducting nanowire with sufficiently small cross section s geometric capacitance (per length) C and kinetic inductance (times length) L kin Below we will demonstrate that -in the setup displayed Fig. 13 -a "disordered" phase λ < 2 (or g < 16) for a superconducting nanowire itself consists of two different phases: A non-superconducting one with g < 2 as well as a "mixed" one with 2 < g < 16 characterized by two different correlation lengths, L c (217) and

The latter phase is characterized by a non-trivial interplay between supercurrent and quantum fluctuations resulting in superconducting behavior of the wire at shorter length scales combined with its vanishing superconducting response in the long scale limit.

div.head: A. Effective action
div.p: Low energy processes in the system under consideration can be described by the effective action
where, as before,
div.formula: S T L [ϕ] = C 8e 2 1/T 0 dτ dx (∂ τ ϕ) 2 + v 2 (∂ x ϕ) 2(220)
div.p: is the low energy effective action for a superconducting nanowire in the ϕ-representation [12][13][14] and
div.formula: S J [ϕ 1 , ϕ 2 ] = -E J 1/T 0 dτ cos(ϕ 1 + φ/2) + cos(ϕ 2 -φ/2)(221)
div.p: accounts for the Josephson energy of the contacts, where we set ϕ 1 = ϕ(0, τ ), ϕ 2 = ϕ(L, τ ).
For simplicity, in Eq. ( 221)) we do not include the charging energy of the point contacts which could always be absorbed into the first term of the wire action (220).

As the wire action (220) is Gaussian it is possible to exactly integrate out the phase variable ϕ(x) at all values x except for x = 0, L. Then we arrive at the reduced effective action S R which depends on the two phase variables ϕ 1 and ϕ 2 . The grand partition function
div.formula: Z reads Z = Dϕ(x) e -S T L [ϕ(x)]-S J [ϕ(0),ϕ(L)] = Dϕ 1 Dϕ 2 e -S R [ϕ 1 ,ϕ 2 ]-S J [ϕ 1 ,ϕ 2 ] ,
div.p: where
Here the trace also includes integration over the imaginary time. The Green function G 0 = ϕ(x, τ )ϕ(0, 0) S 0 has the form
div.formula: G 0 (ω n , x) = 4e 2 C dq 2π e iqx ω 2 n + v 2 q 2 = 4π g|ω n | e -| ωnx v | .(223)
div.p: In order to diagonalize the quadratic part of the action it is convenient to express S R in terms of the variables ϕ ± = (ϕ 1 ± ϕ 2 )/2. Then we obtain
div.formula: S R + S J = 1 2 a=± tr ϕ a G -1 0,a ϕ a -2E J 1/T 0 dτ cos(ϕ + ) cos(ϕ --φ/2)
div.p: with the propagators

Let us stress that the phase variables ϕ + and ϕ -account for different physics in our problem. The phase ϕ -= (ϕ(L) -ϕ(0))/2 determines the supercurrent flowing in-between two contacts inside the wire segment of length L. Hence, configurations with non-zero ϕ - have non-zero energies due the kinetic inductance of the wire and the mode corresponding to ϕ -has a mass equal to gv/2πL. The variable ϕ + , in contrast, describes simultaneous shifts of both phases ϕ(0) and ϕ(L) by the same value without producing any phase gradient along the wire. Thus, in the absence of interactions the mode corresponding to ϕ + is massless.
At the same time, below we will observe that fluctuations of ϕ + yield renormalization of the Josephson coupling energies E J of the contacts and, as such, should also be taken into account.
Let us make use of the variational perturbation theory as described, e.g., in Ref. [72].
The main idea here is to improve the standard perturbation expansion by adding an extra term δS which depends on the variational parameters to the quadratic part of the action S R .
In order to accomplish this goal the partition function ( 222) can be identically rewritten as
div.formula: Z = Dϕ 1 Dϕ 2 e -Str e -(S J -δS)(225)
div.p: with the trial action S tr = S R + δS. The last exponent can then be conveniently expanded in powers of S J -δS. Being expanded to all orders, the partition function (225) obviously remains independent of the choice of δS and the variational parameters. However, such a dependence emerges as long as only a finite number of terms of this expansion is kept. Then the most accurate approximation is achieved by minimizing the result of the perturbative expansion with respect to the variational parameters.
In what follows we choose the trial action in the form
which corresponds to effectively performing a self-consistent harmonic approximation (SCHA).

Here the parameters m ± represent the interaction-generated effective masses for the modes associated with the phase variables ϕ ± . The parameter ψ accounts for the average value of the combination (ϕ(L) -ϕ(0))/2. We note that a somewhat similar variational calculation with a massive term was proposed in the context of Brownian motion of a quantum particle in a periodic potential with linear Ohmic dissipation [73]. The results obtained within the framework of this variational approach agree with those derived by means of more rigorous techniques [41].
Expanding the last exponent in Eq. ( 225) in powers of S J -δS and evaluating the integrals, for the free energy F = -T lnZ we get
div.formula: F = F 0 + F 1 + higher order terms,(227)
div.p: where
div.formula: F 0 = T 2 tr ln G -1 + + tr ln G -1 - , F 1 = S int -δS tr .
div.p: Neglecting all higher order terms in the expansion (227) and evaluating the average in Eq.
(228) with respect to S tr (226), we obtain
where
div.formula: G -1 ± = G -1 0,± + m ± and G ± (0) = T ωn G ± (ω n )
div.p: with ω n = πT (2n + 1) being the Matsubara frequency.
Evaluating variational derivatives of F with respect to m ± and ψ, one readily finds
Imposing the extremum conditions δF/δm ± = δF/δψ = 0 and making use of Eqs. ( 229)-( 232) we arrive at the following set of SCHA equations:
and

Note that the mass parameters m ± in Eq. As we already pointed out, the wire effective action in the form (220) applied in the low energy limit, i.e. for ω, vq ∆. Hence, a proper ultraviolet cutoff should be imposed which respects both causality and the fluctuation-dissipation relation. This goal is achieved by modifying the spectral density
div.formula: J ± (ω) = - 1 π Im G R ± (ω)
div.p: making it decay at ω > ∆. The retarded Green function G R ± (ω) can be obtained from its Matsubara counterpart by means of the standard analytic continuation procedure
div.formula: G R ± (ω) = -G ± (iω n )| iωn→ω+i0 , ω n > 0.
div.p: Then the Matsubara frequency summation in G ± (0) can be performed with the aid of the contour integration in the complex plane.
Employing our regularization procedure we obtain
div.formula: G ± (0) = T ωn G ± (ω n ) = 2π g T ωn |ω n | 1 ± e -|ωnL/v| + µ -1 = 1 4πi C dz G ± (-iz) coth z 2T = i 4π ∞ -∞ dω G R ± (ω) -G A ± (ω) coth ω 2T = ∆ 0 dω J ± (ω) coth ω 2T ,(236)
div.p: where the spectral density functions J ± (ω) read
and we define µ = 2πm/g. In the limit µL/v 1 these expressions reduce to
Let us now evaluate the supercurrent I flowing in the wire segment of length L in-between two Josephson junctions. This current can be defined as
div.formula: I = -2eT 1 Z dZ dφ = 2e dF dφ = -2eE J sin(ψ -φ/2)e -G + (0)+G -(0) /2 = gev 2πL ψ.(239)
div.p: Thus, within the framework of our approach the effect of phase fluctuations is accounted for by effective renormalization of the critical current by the factor e -G + (0)+G -(0) /2 .
In the zero temperature limit T → 0 the solution of Eq. ( 234) takes the form
while the renormalized equation of motion (235) can be rewritten as

We observe that for g < 2 one has ψ = 0 and, hence, the supercurrent I inside the wire is completely suppressed by strong quantum fluctuations of the phase. on the other hand, at bigger values of g > 2 a non-vanishing supercurrent I can flow across the wire segment between the two Josephson junctions.

We arrive at an important conclusion: A quantum phase transition occurs at g = 2 separating two different phases with non-superconducting (g < 2) and superconductinglike (g > 2) behavior. This dissipative QPT belongs to the same universality class as the so-called Schmid phase transition in resistively shunted Josephson junctions [41,74]. It is curious that this QPT occurs at exactly the same value of the parameter g where the superconducting gap singularity in the local electron density of states gets suppressed due to interaction between electrons and a dissipative bath formed by Mooij-Schön plasmons.

Let us now focus our attention on the superconducting-like phase g > 2 and evaluate the supercurrent I affected by quantum fluctuations of the phase ϕ inside the wire. For this purpose let us combine the solution of the equation
div.formula: ∆L v 4πE J g∆ g g-2 sin(ψ -φ/2)[cos(ψ -φ/2)] 2 g-2 + ψ = 0(242)
div.p: with Eq. ( 239). We observe that there exists a new length scale L * in our problem associated with the effective mass. Making use of the Ambegaokar-Baratoff formula for the Josephson coupling energy E J = g N ∆/8 (where g N the dimensionless normal state conductance of each tunnel junction) we can express L * in the form
Here we are merely interested in the case of small-size tunnel junctions with few conducting channels serving as probes aiming to disturb the superconducting wire as little as possible.
Accordingly, one typically has g N g and L * diverges at g → 2 remaining much longer than the characteristic length scale v/∆ at any value of g > 2.

The length scale (243) separates two different fluctuation regimes. For L L * the wire kinetic inductance contribution remains small as compared to that of the Josephson junctions. Then the phase difference across the wire segment in-between the junctions does not fluctuate being equal to ϕ(L) -ϕ(0) = φ. In this case we reproduce the standard mean field current-phase relation
div.formula: I(φ) = gev 4πL φ.(244)
div.p: In the opposite limit L L * the renormalization of E J already becomes important and phase fluctuations tend to suppress the supercurrent flowing inside the wire. In this limit we arrive at the L-independent result
div.formula: I(φ) = gev 2πL * sin φ 2 cos φ 2 2 g-2 . (245
I(φ) ∝ (π -φ) g g-2 .
div.p: We also point out that for L not much smaller than L * the supercurrent I(φ → π) behaves somewhat differently: It vanishes only for 2 < g < 4, whereas at g > 4 one has (106) derived in Sec. V. As we already discussed, this effective action defines the sine-Gordon model which has a QPT at T → 0 and λ = 2 (or g = 16) separating two different phases [12]. Provided g > 16 "positive" and "negative" quantum phase slips are bound in close "neutral" pairs which do not disrupt phase coherence at any relevant scales exceeding the superconducting coherence length ξ. For such values of g QPS effects are irrelevant for any of the above results for the supercurrent I(φ) which remain applicable without any modifications.
div.formula: I(φ → π) ≈ ge∆ 2π ∆L v 2 g-4 πg N 2g g g-4 ,(246) i
div.p: On the other hand, for g < 16 quantum phase slips are no longer bound in pairs. In this phase relevant excitations of our sine-Gordon theory are kinks and anti-kinks (as well as their bound states) with an effective gap in the spectrum [76,77] ∆ ∝ γ

The appearance of this gap for g < 16 (or λ < 2) gives rise to the correlation length L c ∝ 1/ ∆ which, similarly to the case of superconducting nanorings, can be evaluated by equating the renormalized QPS amplitude at this length scale L c γQPS (L c ) (see Eq. ( 116))

to the inductive energy Φ 2 0 /(2L kin L c ) ∼ ξ∆g ξ /L c with some numerical prefactor. Keeping track of all relevant prefactors, in the most relevant limit ξ v/∆ we obtain and (248). The first of these two lengths diverges at one of the phase boundaries g = 2 whereas the second one tends to infinity at another phase boundary g = 16.
div.formula: L c ≈ π 8 √ 2b 1 1-λ/2 ξ exp ag ξ 2 -λ ξ∆ v λ 2-λ ,(248) cf
div.p: Consider the situation with L * < L c , in which case there exist three regimes. At L < L * the supercurrent is strongly affected only by smooth phase fluctuations and not by quantum phase slips. This regime is accounted for by Eq. (245). At L * < L < L c the supercurrent is insensitive to any kind of phase fluctuations and, hence, it is given by a simple mean field formula (244). Finally, for L > L c the supercurrent gets exponentially suppressed by quantum phase slips and similarly to Eq. (119) we have
div.formula: I(φ) ∼ eg ξ ∆ √ L √ ξ v L∆ 3λ 4 exp - 3ag ξ 4 - L L c 1-λ/2
div.p: sin(φ).
In practical terms, the latter regime can be regarded as non-superconducting provided L strongly exceeds L c .

It is also possible to realize the opposite situation with L * > L c , in particular for values of g close to 2. In this case the length L * becomes irrelevant, and one distinguishes only two regimes: L < L c and L > L c . The first one is again superconducting with the supercurrent I(φ) affected by smooth phase fluctuations according to Eq. ( 245), whereas the second regime corresponds to exponential suppression of the supercurrent due to proliferating QPS, cf. Eq. ( 250). No room for the mean field regime (244) exists at L * > L c .

div.head: E. An alternative setup
div.p: As we already emphasized, the setup displayed in Fig. 13 enables one to pass an equilibrium supercurrent across a wire segment of an arbitrary length L without restricting phase fluctuations inside the wire by any means. Below we will consider an alternative setup that allows to effectively restrict the space available for phase fluctuations by "pinning" the superconducting phase ϕ at one point inside the wire [78]. This setup is schematically shown in Fig. 16. As before, it includes a long and thin superconducting nanowire with one of its ends attached to a bulk superconducting reservoir. This reservoir has a form of an open ring whose opposite end is attached to the wire via a small-area tunnel junction at a distance L along the wire. The open ring is pierced by an external magnetic flux Φ which controls the phase difference φ = 2πΦ/Φ 0 between its ends. Accordingly, the phase at the left end of the wire is pinned by the reservoir and is set equal to zero, i.e. ϕ(x = 0) = 0.

We will demonstrate that such topology controlled phase pinning severely enhances the ability of the wire to conduct supercurrent. This effect can be interpreted in terms of the absence of a massless mode responsible for the destruction of superconductivity at g < 2 in the setup of Fig. 16. Instead, the nanowire embedded in our present setup exhibits a transition between "more" and "less" superconducting phases characterized by different types of long-range behavior. Our observable of interest is again the supercurrent I(φ) flowing through the wire segment of length L between the left wire end and the junction. As before, the phase φ is restricted to the interval φ ∈ (-π, π).

In order to proceed we will again describe our system with the aid of the effective action (219), where the wire contribution S T L (220) remains the same, whereas the Josephson term S J now has a somewhat simpler form
div.formula: S J [ϕ(L)] = -E J 1/T 0 dτ cos ϕ(L) -φ(250)
div.p: as compared to Eq. (221).

Our further analysis will be fully analogous to that already carried out above for the setup of Fig. 13. Integrating out the phase variable ϕ(x) at all points along the wire except for its value at x = L we arrive at the reduced effective action
div.formula: S R + S J = 1 2 tr ϕ G -1 0 ϕ -E J 1/T 0 dτ cos(ϕ -φ)(251)
div.p: with
div.formula: G 0 (ω n ) = 8π gω n tanh ω n L v(252)
div.p: We observe that fluctuations of the phase variable are massive with m 0 = gv/8πL. The absence of a massless mode in our setup (in contrast to that of Fig. 13) is a direct consequence of phase pinning at x = 0 which prohibits uniform shifts of the phase inside the wire.
Again employing the SCHA-type of analysis we define the trial action

where the variational parameter m accounts for the interaction-induced effective mass for the ϕ-mode and ψ determines the average value of the phase difference. Evaluating the free energy of the system as a function of these two parameters and minimizing it with respect to both m and ψ, we arrive at the following SCHA equations
div.formula: E J sin(ψ -φ)e -G(0)/2 + gv 8πL ψ = 0,(255)
div.p: where
As before, the effect of phase fluctuations reduces to effective renormalization of E J by the factor e -G(0)/2 . The supercurrent I is then found from the equation
div.formula: I = gev 4πL ψ.(257)
div.p: At L v/∆ phase fluctuations are strongly suppressed and the system remains in the mean-field regime. In the opposite limit of large L the solution of Eq. ( 254) exhibits two qualitatively distinct regimes. At g < 4 we find |m| v/L. Therefore, the emergent mass is negligible, and the effect of fluctuations is purely Gaussian. The equation of motion ( 255) is then rewritten as
div.formula: E J sin(ψ -φ) ∆L v -4/g + gv 8πL ψ = 0.(258)
div.p: In the interesting for us limit of small E J we may readily set 8πE J g∆ < 1. In this case the sine term is renormalized to zero faster than the kinetic inductance contribution ∝ L -1 and, hence, we obtain
div.formula: I(φ) = 2eE J v ∆L 4/g sin φ.(259)
div.p: This expression demonstrates that for g < 4 phase fluctuations (i) modify the currentphase relation making it sine-like instead of the sawtooth-like and (ii) yield a decrease of the supercurrent as compared to the standard Josephson formula I(φ) = 2eE J sin φ that applies in the limit L → 0. In addition, we observe that in the presence of fluctuations the supercurrent (259) decays faster with increasing L than the standard mean field dependence
Let us now turn to the case g > 4. Resolving Eq. (254) in the limit L → ∞ we obtain
div.formula: m =      E J cos(ψ -φ) 8π ∆g 4 g g g-4 , cos(ψ -φ) > 0, -gv/8πL + o(1/L), cos(ψ -φ) < 0.(260)
div.p: This solution remains valid only as long as L exceeds the length scale
div.formula: L * = v ∆ g πg N g g-4 ,(261)
div.p: This length separates the regime L > L * where fluctuations yield non-Gaussian renormalization of the interaction potential from the Gaussian regime L L * where |m| gv/8πL.
As long as v/∆ L L * the current is again given by Eq. (259).

div.head: F. Discussion
div.p: The above analysis demonstrates that superconducting properties of metallic nanowires depend not only on their parameters, but also on the topology of the experimental setup and A. The model Consider the system of two long parallel to each other superconducting nanowires, as it is schematically shown in Fig. 17. As before, the wires are described by geometric capacitances C 1 and C 2 (per unit wire length) and kinetic inductances L 1 and L 2 (times length) effectively representing the two transmission lines. Capacitive coupling between these two nanowires is accounted for by the mutual capacitance C m . Generalizing our analysis in Sec. IVA to the above structure, we arrive at the contribution to the system Hamiltonian that keeps track of both electric and magnetic energies in these coupled transmission lines. It reads [88,89]
div.formula: C m v 2 v 2 v 1 v 1 Phase Slip: γ 2 Phase Slip: 1 γ C 1 ,L 1 C 2 ,L 2
ĤTL = 1 2 i,j=1,2 dx(L -1 ij Φi (x) Φj (x) + (1/Φ 2 0 )C -1 ij (∂ x χi (x)∂ x χj (x)),(262)
div.p: where x is the coordinate along the wires, L ij and C ij denote the matrix elements of the inductance and capacitance matrices
As before, the Hamiltonian (262) is expressed in terms of the dual operators χ(x) and Φ(x)
which obey the canonical commutation relation
div.formula: [ Φi (x), χj (x )] = -iδ ij Φ 0 δ(x -x ).(264)
div.p: Provided the wires are thick enough the low energy Hamiltonian in Eq. ( 262) is sufficient.
However, for thinner wires one should also account for the effect of quantum phase slips.
The corresponding contribution to the total Hamiltonian for our system can be expressed in the form (cf., Eq. ( 79))
div.formula: ĤQPS = - j=1,2 γ j dx cos( χj (x)),(265)
div.p: where
div.formula: γ j ∼ (g jξ ∆/ξ) exp(-ag jξ ), a ∼ 1, j = 1, 2(266)
div.p: are the QPS amplitudes per unit wire length, ∆ is the superconducting order parameter in each of the wires, g jξ = R q /R jξ and R jξ is the normal state resistance of the j-th wire segment of length equal to the superconducting coherence length ξ.
The total Hamiltonian for the system under consideration equals to the sum of the two terms in Eqs. ( 262) and ( 265),
div.formula: Ĥ = ĤTL + ĤQPS ,(267)
div.p: representing an effective sine-Gordon model that will be treated below.

div.head: B. Plasma mode splitting
div.p: Any QPS event causes redistribution of charges inside the wire and generates a pair of voltage pulses propagating simultaneously in the opposite directions along the wire with the velocity v, see also Fig. 18 (left panel). In a single wire this process is controlled, e.g., by the wave equation (186) for the operator χ. In the case of two capacitively coupled wires a QPS event in one of the wires generates voltage pulses in both wires. The corresponding generalization of the wave equation (186) for the operators χ1,2 in each of the two wires follows directly from the system Hamiltonian (262), and the corresponding voltage operators
div.formula: V1,2 read Vi (t) = 1/Φ 0 j=1,2 C -1 ij (∂ x χj (x 1 , t) -∂ x χj (x 2 , t)).(268)
div.p: It will be convenient for us to go over to the phase representation and express the corresponding equation of motion for the phases ϕ 1,2 in both wires in the form (cf., e.g., Eq.( 85))

where V = ( Č Ľ) -1/2 is the velocity matrix that accounts for plasmon modes propagating along the wires. Assume now that a QPS event occurs at the initial time moment t = 0 at the point x = 0 inside the first wire. As we just discussed, at t > 0 voltage pulses originating from this QPS event will propagate in both wires. Resolving Eq. ( 269) together with the proper initial condition corresponding to a QPS event (cf., e.g., Eq. ( 86)), we arrive at the following picture.

In the first wire each of the two voltage pulses propagating in opposite directions turns out to be split into two pulses of the same sign propagating with different velocities v + and v -, as it is also illustrated in Fig. 18 (middle panel). Each of the voltage pulses generated in the second wire by a QPS event in the first one is also split into two, they also propagate with two different velocities v + and v -, however the signs of these voltage pulses are now opposite to each other, cf. Fig. 18 (right panel). The velocities of these split plasmon modes are determined by the eigenvalues of the velocity matrix V. They read [90] v ± = 1 2κ

This expression indicates that one of these velocities may strongly increase provided the wires are located close enough to each other in which case the cross-capacitance C m may become of order C. In the absence of inter-wire interaction we have κ = 1 and Eq. ( 270) obviously yields v + = v 1 and v -= v 2 .
C. Quantum phase transitions: renormalization group analysis

Let us now turn to the issue of a superconductor-insulator QPT in capacitively coupled superconducting nanowires with proliferating quantum phase slips. As we just demonstrated, the presence of capacitive coupling between the nanowires may significantly affect plasmon propagation in our system and, hence, alter the interaction between quantum phase slips.
In this case, SIT in each of the wires is controlled not only by the parameters of the corresponding wire but also by those of the neighboring one as well as by the mutual capacitance.
Interestingly enough, superconducting nanowires with properly chosen parameters may turn insulating once they are brought sufficiently close to each other [89].

In order to quantitatively describe QPT in coupled superconducting nanowires we will employ the renormalization group (RG) analysis. This approach is well developed and was successfully applied to a variety of problems in condensed matter theory, such as, e.g., the problem of weak Coulomb blockade in tunnel [41,[80][81][82] and non-tunnel [83][84][85] barriers between normal metals or that of a dissipative phase transition in resistively shunted Josephson junctions [41,74,86,87]. In the case of superconducting nanowires QPT was described [12] with the aid of RG equations equivalent to those initially developed for two-dimensional superconducting films [11] which exhibit classical BKT phase transition driven by temperature. In contrast, as we already discussed above, quantum SIT in quasi-one dimensional superconducting wires is controlled by the parameter λ proportional to the square root of the wire cross section s.

Provided the two superconducting wires depicted in Fig. 17 are decoupled from each other, i.e. for C m → 0, one should expect two independent QPT to occur in these two wires respectively at λ 1 = 2 and at λ 2 = 2, where λ 1,2 = (R q /8) C 1,2 /L 1,2 . In the presence of capacitive coupling between the wires these two QPT get modified. In order to account for these modifications let us express the grand partition function of our system Z = Tr exp(-Ĥ/T ) in terms of the path integral
where
div.formula: S = 1 2Φ 2 0 i,j=1,2 dxdτ ξ∆L ij ∂ τ χ i ∂ τ χ j + 1 ξ∆ C -1 ij ∂ x χ i ∂ x χ j - i=1,2 y i dxdτ cos(χ i )(273)
div.p: is the effective action corresponding to the Hamiltonian (267) and y i = γ i ξ/∆ denote the effective fugacity for the gas of quantum phase slips in the i-th wire. For the sake of convenience in Eq. ( 273) we rescaled the spatial coordinate in units of x 0 , i.e. x → xξ and the time coordinate in units of τ 0 , i.e. τ → τ /∆.
As usually, let us divide the χ-variables into fast and slow components χ i = χ f i + χ s i , where
div.formula: χ f i (x, τ ) =
div.p: Λ<ω 2 +q 2 <Λ+δΛ dωdq 2π χ ω,q e iωτ +iqx , χ s i (x, τ ) = ω 2 +q 2 <Λ dωdq 2π χ ω,q e iωτ +iqx .

Assuming δΛ/Λ 1, expanding in χ f i and integrating these fast variables out we employ the perturbation theory in y 1,2 and observe that in order to account for the leading order corrections it suffices to evaluate the following matrix Green function at coincident points
where λ = (R q /8) V Č. The matrix λ reads λ = 1
Following the standard analysis [11] and proceeding to bigger and bigger scales Λ, we eventually arrive at the following RG equations for the QPS fugacities y 1 and y 2 :
div.formula: dy i d log Λ = (2 -λ ii )y i , i = 1, 2,(276)
div.p: where λ 11 and λ 22 are diagonal elements of the matrix λ. Note that here we restrict our RG analysis to the lowest order in y 1,2 , in which case other parameters of our problem remain unrenormalized.
It follows immediately from Eqs. (276) that our system exhibits two BKT-like QPT at λ 11 = 2 and λ 22 = 2. For the first wire the corresponding phase transition point is fixed by the condition [89]
The same condition for the second wire is derived from Eq. It is fairly obvious that capacitive coupling depends on the distance between the wires.

While at large distances this coupling is negligible, as the wires get closer to each other the value C m increases and, hence, their mutual influence increases as well. Let us choose the wire parameters in such a way that for C m = 0 both λ 1 and λ 2 slightly exceed 2, i.e. the wires remain in the superconducting phase being relatively close to SIT. Moving the wires closer to each other we "turn on" capacitive coupling between them and, hence, decrease both values λ 1 and λ 2 below 2. As a result, two superconducting wires become insulating as soon as they are brought sufficiently close to each other. This remarkable physical phenomenon is illustrated by the phase diagram in Fig. 19.
We can also add that transport properties can be investigated in exactly the same manner as in the case of a single nanowire. Generalization of the technique [12] is straightforward and yields
where R i (T ) is the linear resistance of the i-th wire. This result remains applicable either for λ ii > 2 or for any λ ii at sufficiently high temperatures.

The effects discussed here can be observed in a variety of structures involving superconducting nanowires. For instance, superconducting nanowires in the form of a meander are quite frequently employed in experiments, see, e.g., Ref. [91]. Different segments of such a wire remain parallel to each other being close enough to develop electromagnetic coupling.
One can expect, therefore, that the wire of such a geometry should be "less superconducting" than the same wire that has the form of a straight line.
Let us first mimic the behavior of the wire depicted in Fig. 20

and the summation runs over the indices i, j = 1, 2, 3. Proceeding along the same lines as above we again arrive at Eq. ( 274), where the diagonal elements of the matrix λ now read [89] The RG equation ( 276) with i = 2 combined with Eq. (280) also accounts for QPT in the wire having the form of a meander displayed in Fig. 20. In this case, within the Direct experimental evidence for this kind of behavior and for the presence of this localization length was very recently observed in nominally uniform titanium nanowires [92].

Moreover, reanalyzing similar data reported previously [24,25,93] for a large number of M oGe nanowires we conclude that these data are also consistent with the above physical picture involving the correlation length L c (248), i.e. the superconducting M oGe samples [24,25,93] obey the condition L L c , whereas the non-superconducting ones typically have the length L exceeding L c . It is also important to emphasize that the nanowires employed in all these experiments did not contain any grains or dielectric barriers. Hence, similarly to normal metallic structures [1] these observations can be interpreted as a manifestation of weak Coulomb blockade of Cooper pairs that may occur even in the absence of any tunnel barriers.

Further non-trivial properties of superconducting nanowires may be sensitive to specific topology of the experimental setup under consideration. A number of interesting phenomena associated with quantum phase slips also occurs in capacitively coupled superconducting nanowires.

Finally, it is worthwhile to point out that non-trivial quantum properties of superconducting nanowires and nanorings open up plenty of possibilities for their applications in nanoelectronics, metrology and quantum information technology. Various devices, such as, e.g., single-charge transistor [48] and charge quantum interference device [49] have already been demonstrated. Superconducting nanowires can also be employed as central elements for QPS flux qubits [53] as well as for creating a QPS-based standard of electric current [50] and single photon detectors [91]. We are confident that intensive investigations of intriguing fundamental properties of quasi-one-dimensional superconducting structures as well as their technological applications will continue in the near future.

IV. QUANTUM PHASE SLIPS AND PHASE-CHARGE DUALITYAs we already discussed, at low temperatures the most significant non-Gaussian quantum fluctuations in superconducting nanowires are quantum phase slips. Provided such a wire is sufficiently thin quantum fluctuations may yield temporal local suppression of the absolute value of the superconducting order parameter field ∆(x) = |∆(x)|e iϕ(x) in different points along the wire. As soon as the modulus of the order parameter |∆(x)| in the point x vanishes, the phase ϕ(x) becomes unrestricted and can jump by the value ±2π. After this process the modulus |∆(x)| gets restored, the phase becomes single valued again and the system returns to its initial state accumulating the net phase shift ±2π.

is attached to two big superconducting reservoirs at its ends. In order to probe fluctuation effects inside the wire it is connected to a bulk superconductor forming an open ring by two identical small area tunnel junctions with Josephson energy E J located at a distance L from each other at the points x = 0 and x = L. External magnetic flux Φ piercing the ring controls the phase difference φ = 2πΦ/Φ 0 between the bulk sides of the point contacts. As desired, within this setup fluctuations of the superconducting phase ϕ(x, τ ) remain unrestricted in any point x of the wire. Below we will analyze the effect of these fluctuations on the supercurrent I(φ) flowing through the wire segment of length L between the two Josephson contacts[71]. As I(φ) is a 2π-periodic function of φ in what follows it suffices to restrict the phase interval to |φ| ≤ π.

(233) remain equal to each other since the two Josephson junctions involved in our problem are identical. Equation (234) provides the relation between the effective mass m and the fluctuation-induced renormalization of the Josephson coupling energy E J . Equation (235) represents the equation of motion for ψ. It coincides with the equation of motion for the phase ϕ -with E J renormalized by quantum fluctuations.

Comparing the results (244) and (245) we observe that quantum fluctuations can strongly affect both the supercurrent magnitude and the current-phase relation. The dependence I(φ) (245) in the presence of fluctuations becomes smoother than in Eq. (244) and the absolute value of the supercurrent is reduced by the factor ∼ L/L * . Additional reduction of I originates from the term in the square brackets in Eq. (245): The supercurrent gets suppressed stronger for bigger values of φ The latter effect becomes particularly significant for g sufficiently close to 2. For φ → π and any g > 2 the supercurrent tends to zero as

.e. for such values of g the current-phase relation remains discontinuous at φ = π. The dependencies I(φ) evaluated for different values of g and L are also displayed in Fig.14.Finally, we note that the form of the current-phase relation (245) derived here resembles that obtained for resistively shunted Josephson junctions in the presence of quantum fluctuations of the phase[75].D. Effect of QPS and localization of Cooper pairsLet us recall that the effective action S T L (220) employed in the above analysis only accounts for the effect of Gaussian fluctuations of the superconducting phase and does not yet include quantum phase slips. In order to describe QPS effects inside the wire it is convenient to turn to the dual χ-representation for the wire effective action S eff [J(τ ) = 0]

figure.head: Figure 15 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 15. Localization of Cooper pairs in the ground state of a uniform superconducting nanowire due to strong zero-point fluctuations of magnetic flux.

figure.head: Figure 16 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 16. A superconducting nanowire directly attached to a bulk open superconducting ring at its left end. The ring is also attached to the wire via a small-area tunnel junction at a distance L from its left end. The open ring is pierced by an external magnetic flux Φ.

figure.head: For g > 4
figure.figDesc: the renormalized Josephson coupling energy decreases slower than 1/L and at L ∼ L * it becomes of the same order as the kinetic inductance contribution. At even larger distances mass renormalization saturates to the value defined in Eq. (260). The kinetic inductance contribution, on the contrary, decreases as 1/L. Therefore, at L L * the phase is pinned to the lowest minimum of the renormalized Josephson junction potential, i.e. we have ψ = φ. In this case the current-phase relation reduces to the standard mean field form (244).Finally, we note that the effects of QPS inside the superconducting nanowire are analyzed in exactly the same manner as it was already done above in Sec. IXD, except one should now distinguish two phasesg < 4 and 4 < g < 16 (instead of g < 2 and 2 < g < 16) -and replace the expression for L * (243) by Eq. (261).For g < 4 there exists only one correlation length (248) in our problem. At L L cQPS effects are irrelevant and the supercurrent suppression is merely due to smooth phase fluctuations. In this limit Eq. (259) applies and the supercurrent decays as a power-law with increasing L. As soon as L exceeds L c quantum phase slips come into play and the supercurrent decay becomes exponential with L according to Eq. (250). At 4 < g < 16 there already exist two different correlation lengths, L * and L c . The first one diverges as g → 4 while the second one tends to infinity at g → 16. Depending on the relation between these two lengths, different regimes can occur. Consider, e.g., the limit L * L c which can always be realized for sufficiently large values of g ξ . In this case, at shorter length scales L < L * only smooth phase fluctuations affect the supercurrent causing its power-law suppression with increasing L and the sinusoidal current-phase relation, see Eq. (259). At L * < L < L c both smooth phase fluctuations and quantum phase slips are irrelevant and the supercurrent is defined by the standard mean field result I ∝ 1/L (244) describing the sawtooth-shaped current-phase relation. Finally, at L L c the current is exponentially suppressed and the current-phase relation again reduces to the sine form (250).

Right panel: ime-dependent phase configurations at t = 0 (red) and t > 0 (blue) together with propagating voltage pulses in the second of the two capacitively coupled superconducting nanowires generated by a QPS event in the first one.

figure.figDesc: These results allow to conclude that in the presence of capacitive coupling quantum fluctuations in one of these wires tend to decrease superconducting properties of the other one. As a result, SIT in both wires occurs at larger values of λ 1,2 than in the absence of such coupling. Equation (277) demonstrates that the magnitude of this effect depends on the ratio of the plasmon velocities in the two wires v 1 /v 2 and on the strength of the capacitive coupling controlled by C m . Provided the wire cross sections s 1 and s 2 differ strongly the plasmon velocities v i ∝ √ s i also differ considerably. Provided, e.g., the first wire is much thinner than the second one we have v 1 v 2 and, hence, the QPT condition (277) in the first wire remains almost unaffected for any capacitive coupling strength. If, on the contrary, the first wire is much thicker than the second one, then one has v 1 v 2 and the condition (277) reduces to λ 1 2/ 1 -C 2 m /(C 1 C 2 ) implying that the critical value λ 1 can exceed 2 considerably for sufficiently large values C m .

where the function P (x, x ; t, t ) is defined in P (x, x ; t, t ) = e i( χ+ (x,t)-χ+ (x ,t )-

Equation (180) enables one to directly evaluate all voltage correlators by taking variational derivatives of W with respect to J(t). It follows immediately from Eq. (180) that in the absence of QPS all voltage cumulants except for the second one (describing Gaussian noise) vanish identically. We conclude, therefore, that at low enough temperatures only quantum phase slips give rise to both shot noise of the voltage and to all higher cumulants of the voltage operator in superconducting nanowires.
We also note that in the case of a constant in time current bias I we have χ 0 (x, t) = IΦ 0 t and the function P depends only on the time difference, i.e. P (x, x ; t, t ) = P (x, x ; t -t ).
This property will be employed in our subsequent calculations.

div.head: B. Voltage cumulants in the zero frequency limit
div.p: As a first step, let us make use of the above general results and evaluate all cumulants of the voltage operator the zero frequency limit. As we already know, an instantaneous voltage value V (t) fluctuates in time due to a sequence of voltage pulses produced by QPS. It is on the way the experiment is being performed. The ability of the wire to carry supercurrent also varies at different length scales being affected by different kinds of fluctuations including, on one hand, sound-like collective plasma modes forming a quantum dissipative environment for electrons inside the wire and, on the other hand, quantum phase slips. As the bath of plasma modes is (almost) Ohmic the low temperature system behavior resembles that involving a Schmid-like dissipative QPT [41,74] either at g = 2 or at g = 4 depending on the setup under consideration. The presence of quantum phase slips naturally leads to a BKT-type quantum phase transition at g = 16 [12].
While the system displayed in Fig. 13 allows for unrestricted fluctuations of the superconducting phase, the setup of Fig. 16 effectively pins the phase at one of the wire ends.

In the former case a gapless Ohmic mode (associated with uniform phase shifts along the wire) appears at any L. Fluctuations associated with this gapless mode cause a Schmidlike QPT at g = 2. As a result, the wire completely loses superconductivity at g < 2,

whereas the phase with 2 < g < 16 is mixed, i.e. it is non-superconducting in the long length limit and superconducting at shorter scales, even though the gapless mode causes additional suppression of current in the limit L → 0. Comparing this situation with the one encountered for the structure of Fig. 16, we observe that in the latter case superconductivity is severely enhanced as a result of the soft mode suppression due to phase pinning. This effect turns the QPT into a transition between "less" and "more" superconducting phases at g = 4 . Note that a similar phase transition was also discussed in Ref. [79] in the context of superconducting nanorings interrupted by a Josephson junction.

div.head: IX. QUANTUM PHASE SLIPS IN CAPACITIVELY COUPLED SUPERCONDUCTING NANOWIRES
div.p: Let us now extend our analysis of QPS-related effects to yet another structure which consists of capacitively coupled superconducting nanowires. We will demonstrate that quantum fluctuations in one of the two wires effectively "add up" to those of another one even without any direct electric contact between them, thereby giving rise to a number of interesting effects, such as, e.g., splitting of plasmon modes and interaction-induced SIT shifting in each of the wires. approximation of the nearest neighbor capacitive interaction between the wire segments QPT occurs at

i.e. the critical value of the parameter λ exceeds 2 for any C m = 0. The approximation of the nearest neighbor interaction appears to be well justified in the limit C m C. For stronger interactions with C m ∼ C this approximation becomes insufficient for a quantitative analysis. Qualitatively, our key observations should remain applicable also in this case: A nanowire in the form of a straight line with λ slightly exceeding the critical value 2 should demonstrate superconducting-like behavior with R(T ) ∝ T 2λ-3 [12] whereas the wire with exactly the same parameters may turn insulating provided it has the form of a meander with capacitive coupling between its segments.

div.head: X. CONCLUDING REMARKS
div.p: In this review we have made an effort to cover a number of recent developments related to quantum properties of superconducting nanowires putting an emphasis on fundamental aspects of the theory of such systems. Unlike in the case of bulk superconductors, the low temperature physics of quasi-one-dimensional nanowires and nanorings is essentially determined by quantum fluctuations which are, in turn, controlled by two different parameters, the dimensionless normal state conductance of the wire segment g ξ (2) and the dimensionless wire admittance g (3). Both these parameters decrease with decreasing wire cross section making quantum fluctuations progressively more pronounced.

Provided the parameter g ξ remains very large, the low temperature behavior of the system is determined by small (Gaussian) quantum fluctuations of the phase of the order parameter which -for not very large values of g -significantly affect the electron density of states in superconducting nanowires and cause supercurrent noise in superconducting nanorings.

As soon as the dimensionless conductance g ξ becomes not too large quantum phase slips come into play. As a result, current-biased superconducting nanowires acquire a non-zero resistance and exhibit shot noise of the voltage. These phenomena can be conveniently interpreted in terms of tunneling of quantum fluxons (i.e. the flux quanta Φ 0 ) across the nanowire. In the context of phase-charge duality, these fluxons can be treated as effective quantum "particles" exactly dual to Cooper pairs with charge 2e. Such "particles" obey complicated full counting statistics which, however, reduces to Poissonian one in the zero frequency limit.

Quantum phase slips may strongly affect both the supercurrent and its fluctuations in superconducting nanorings. This effect can be particularly pronounced if QPS remain unbound, i.e. for g < 16 or, equivalently, for λ < 2. In this case and provided the ring radius exceeds the critical value R c (118), even at T → 0 strong quantum fluctuations essentially "dephase" and suppress supercurrent that could flow across the ring.
The same non-perturbative length scale L c (248) emerges in superconducting nanowires.

For g < 16 and T → 0 such nanowires do show an insulating behavior at scales exceeding typical size of a "superconducting domain" L c , whereas at shorter length scales they may exhibit superconducting properties albeit possibly affected by quantum fluctuations of the phase. Hence, L c can be interpreted as localization length for Cooper pairs.
